title: Saying No: Negation & Dislikes
cefrDescriptors: Can make simple negative statements about activities and preferences.
Can express dislikes using ne… pas and détester.
concept:
So far you've said what you do and like. Now learn to say what you DON'T — which, honestly, is half of any real conversation.

French negation is a sandwich: it wraps the conjugated verb in two words, ne … pas.
  je parle  ->  je ne parle pas
  tu aimes  ->  tu n'aimes pas
  il regarde -> il ne regarde pas

The rule: put ne BEFORE the verb and pas AFTER it. Both pieces are required — using only one ("je parle pas" / "je ne parle") is incomplete in careful French, and the DELF expects the full ne … pas.

One spelling change: ne becomes n' before a vowel or a silent h, exactly like je -> j'. So « je ne aime pas » is wrong; it's je n'aime pas. Same with n'habite pas, n'écoute pas.

For strong feelings, use détester (to hate / really dislike), a normal -er verb: je déteste les légumes. That gives you a three-point scale:
  j'aime  (I like)  <  je n'aime pas  (I don't like)  <  je déteste (I hate).

A heads-up for listening: in casual speech, French speakers often drop the ne and just say « j'aime pas ». You'll hear it constantly — but keep writing the full ne … pas yourself, especially in the exam.

Write three true negative sentences about yourself: something you don't speak, don't watch, and don't like.

vocabulary.exampleFr: Je ne mange pas de viande.
Je ne mange pas de viande.

examples.fr: Nous ne regardons pas la télé.
J'aime le thé, mais je n'aime pas le café.
examples.en: I like tea, but I don't like coffee.
